13	//   - document extraction source-ID guidance: sourceMessageId can be a
14	//     document chunk ID, and document evidence must quote from one chunk.
15	//
16	// Version 2.0.1 added:
17	//   - explicit meaning-based domain classification guidance, including
18	//     `general` for cross-functional/end-to-end business process flow claims.
19	//
20	// Version 2.0.0 added:
21	//   - sensitivityFlags — strict-mode HR/PII/personal-conflict detection so the
22	//     candidate-before-claim sensitivity gate can actually fire in production
23	//     (P1 #2 from the external code review).
24	//   - proposedEntities — model-surfaced entity references (customers, licensors,
25	//     factories, systems, etc.) so R5.5's licensor-vs-vendor resolver actually
26	//     trips in production (P2 #1 from the same review).
27	//
28	// Do NOT edit the prompt text without bumping EXTRACTION_PROMPT_VERSION and
29	// logging the change in DECISIONS.md.
30	
31	import { z } from 'zod';
32	import { KNOWLEDGE_DOMAINS, ENTITY_TYPES } from '@oracle/shared';
33	
34	export const EXTRACTION_PROMPT_VERSION = '2.2.0';
35	
36	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
37	// Claim type taxonomy (spec 9.4 + Part 6).
38	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
39	export const CLAIM_TYPES = [
40	  'process_rule',      // How something normally works
41	  'exception_rule',    // An exception to a standard process
42	  'bottleneck',        // A constraint or delay point (person, system, or approval)
43	  'workaround',        // An unofficial solution for a missing capability
44	  'system_limitation', // What a system (Coldlion, ERP, etc.) fails to do
45	  'dependency',        // Something that must occur before something else
46	  'handoff_gap',       // Information lost between departments
47	  'contradiction',     // Two conflicting facts about the same process
48	  'process_ambiguity', // Unclear ownership or unclear when/how something happens
49	] as const;
50	
51	export type ClaimType = (typeof CLAIM_TYPES)[number];
52	
53	// Group chat semantic roles (spec 9.5).
54	export const SEMANTIC_ROLES = [
55	  'claim_stated',
56	  'claim_confirmed',
57	  'claim_challenged',
58	  'claim_refined',
59	  'exception_introduced',
60	  'process_ambiguity_revealed',
61	] as const;
62	
63	export type SemanticRole = (typeof SEMANTIC_ROLES)[number];
64	
65	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
66	// Zod output schema for generateObject calls.
67	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
68	
69	export const ExtractionEvidenceSchema = z.object({
70	  exactQuote: z
71	    .string()
72	    .min(5)
73	    .describe(
74	      'Verbatim substring from the source message — must appear character-for-character in the message content. Do not paraphrase.',
75	    ),
76	  sourceMessageId: z
77	    .string()
78	    .describe(
79	      'The ID of the source this quote was taken from. For chat extraction, this must be one of the provided message IDs. For document extraction, this must be one of the provided document chunk IDs.',
80	    ),
81	  confidence: z
82	    .number()
83	    .int()
84	    .min(1)
85	    .max(10)
86	    .describe('Confidence that this quote supports the claim (1=uncertain, 10=certain).'),
87	});
88	
89	export const ExtractionGapSchema = z.object({
90	  questionToAsk: z
91	    .string()
92	    .min(10)
93	    .describe('A follow-up question that would resolve an uncertainty revealed in this segment.'),
94	  whyItMatters: z
95	    .string()
96	    .min(10)
97	    .describe('Why resolving this gap is operationally important for POP Creations / Spruce Line.'),
98	  priority: z.enum(['low', 'medium', 'high', 'urgent']),
99	});
100	
101	/**
102	 * R5.5 — Entity reference the model surfaced from the text. The validator
103	 * resolves these against the canonical entity registry; the licensor-vs-
104	 * vendor type-mismatch check fires here if the model proposes a known
105	 * licensor under `entity_type = 'vendor'`.
106	 */
107	export const ExtractionEntityProposalSchema = z.object({
108	  entityType: z
109	    .enum(ENTITY_TYPES as unknown as [string, ...string[]])
110	    .describe(
111	      'The kind of entity. licensor is for entertainment-IP rights holders (Disney, Marvel, Star Wars, Warner Bros, NBCUniversal) — NEVER use vendor for those. vendor is the residual bucket for non-customer non-factory non-licensor business partners.',
112	    ),
113	  rawString: z
114	    .string()
115	    .min(1)
116	    .max(255)
117	    .describe(
118	      'The entity name as it appears in the message (the validator resolves aliases against the canonical registry).',
119	    ),
120	});
121	
122	/**
123	 * R5.5 / P1 #2 — Sensitivity flags. STRICT mode: only flag content that is
124	 * clearly disciplinary, medical, compensation, or hostile interpersonal.
125	 * Operational mentions of named employees are NOT sensitive — e.g. "Jordan
126	 * handles licensing" is fine. The flag only fires when the content itself
127	 * describes personnel/HR/medical/conflict material that shouldn't live in
128	 * the operational knowledge graph.
129	 *
130	 * Setting any of these true causes the candidate to be quarantined and
131	 * never promoted into the `claims` table.
132	 */
133	export const ExtractionSensitivityFlagsSchema = z.object({
134	  containsSensitiveHRData: z
135	    .boolean()
136	    .describe(
137	      'True ONLY if the message contains: formal disciplinary action (warning, PIP, termination), compensation specifics (salary numbers, bonus amounts, raise discussions), formal performance reviews/ratings, or formally documented LOA reasons (medical leave, family leave). NOT true for casual operational mentions of who is on vacation, who handles a process, or who is out.',
138	    ),
139	  containsSensitivePersonalData: z
140	    .boolean()
141	    .describe(
142	      'True ONLY if the message contains explicit personal information: medical conditions or diagnoses, family situations (divorce, deaths, dependents), legal issues (lawsuits, arrests, immigration status), home addresses or personal contact details, or other clearly private personal facts unrelated to operational work.',
143	    ),
144	  isPersonalConflict: z
145	    .boolean()
146	    .describe(
147	      'True ONLY if the message describes explicit interpersonal hostility BETWEEN NAMED INDIVIDUALS: shouting matches, harassment accusations, character attacks, or formal complaints filed against another employee. NOT true for normal disagreement about a process or generic team frustration.',
148	    ),
149	  sensitivityReason: z
150	    .string()
151	    .max(500)
152	    .optional()
153	    .describe(
154	      'If any of the three flags is true, briefly explain which specific spans triggered the flag. Omit if all three are false.',
155	    ),
156	});
157	
158	export const ExtractionClaimSchema = z.object({
159	  claimType: z.enum(CLAIM_TYPES),
160	  summary: z
161	    .string()
162	    .min(10)
163	    .max(1000)
164	    .describe(
165	      'One declarative sentence stating what this claim asserts about company operations. Should be independently comprehensible without the source messages.',
166	    ),
167	  impactScore: z
168	    .number()
169	    .int()
170	    .min(1)
171	    .max(10)
172	    .describe('Business impact if this claim is true (1=trivial curiosity, 10=critical blocker).'),
173	  confidenceScore: z
174	    .number()
175	    .int()
176	    .min(1)
177	    .max(10)
178	    .describe(
179	      'How confident you are that this is a reliable, well-supported operational claim (1=speculative, 10=certain from explicit statement).',
180	    ),
181	  domains: z
182	    .array(z.enum(KNOWLEDGE_DOMAINS as unknown as [string, ...string[]]))
183	    .min(1)
184	    .describe(
185	      'Which knowledge domains this claim belongs to (at least one required). Use general for cross-functional, end-to-end, or whole-company process-flow claims that explain how work moves across departments.',
186	    ),
187	  evidence: ExtractionEvidenceSchema,
188	  semanticRole: z
189	    .enum(SEMANTIC_ROLES)
190	    .optional()
191	    .describe(
192	      'In group chats: how this claim was introduced. Omit for single-speaker segments.',
193	    ),
194	  requiresReview: z
195	    .boolean()
196	    .describe(
197	      'Set true if any of: impact >= 7, the claim names a specific person as a bottleneck, or the claim implies customer or licensor risk.',
198	    ),
199	  proposedEntities: z
200	    .array(ExtractionEntityProposalSchema)
201	    .optional()
202	    .describe(
203	      'Distinct entities (customers, licensors, factories, systems, departments, geographies, etc.) referenced in this claim. Omit if no proper-noun entities are mentioned.',
204	    ),
205	  sensitivityFlags: ExtractionSensitivityFlagsSchema.optional().describe(
206	    'Set when any of the three flags is true. Omit when nothing is sensitive (the common case for operational claims).',
207	  ),
208	  suggestedGaps: z
209	    .array(ExtractionGapSchema)
210	    .optional()
211	    .describe(
212	      'Follow-up questions that would resolve uncertainties or ambiguities revealed in this segment.',
213	    ),
214	});
215	
216	export const ExtractionOutputSchema = z.object({
217	  claims: z
218	    .array(ExtractionClaimSchema)
219	    .describe('All operational claims extracted from this conversation segment. Empty array if no claims found.'),
220	  segmentSummary: z
221	    .string()
222	    .max(2000)
223	    .optional()
224	    .describe(
225	      'Brief (1–2 sentence) summary of what this conversation segment covered operationally. Keep it concise — well under 2000 characters. Omit if the segment was trivial or off-topic.',
226	    ),
227	});
228	
229	export type ExtractionOutput = z.infer<typeof ExtractionOutputSchema>;
230	export type ExtractionClaim = z.infer<typeof ExtractionClaimSchema>;
231	export type ExtractionSensitivityFlags = z.infer<typeof ExtractionSensitivityFlagsSchema>;
232	export type ExtractionEntityProposal = z.infer<typeof ExtractionEntityProposalSchema>;
233	
234	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
235	// System prompt text.
236	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
237	
238	export const EXTRACTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT = `You are an operational knowledge extractor for The Oracle.
239	
240	The Oracle is an AI knowledge graph mapping the "dark matter" of POP Creations / Spruce Line — a high-volume home decor company (Brooklyn, NY). They design in-house, manufacture in China, and sell to Burlington, TJX, Ross, Hobby Lobby, Walmart, and others. They use an ERP system called Coldlion. They license from Disney, Marvel, Star Wars / Lucasfilm, Warner Bros, and NBCUniversal.
241	
242	YOUR TASK: Analyze the provided conversation segment and extract concrete, evidence-backed operational claims about how this company actually works. For each claim, also surface:
243	  (1) the entities it references (customers, licensors, factories, systems, departments, geographies),
244	  (2) whether the source content is sensitive in a way that should keep the claim OUT of the operational knowledge graph.
245	
246	CLAIM TYPES:
247	- process_rule: How something normally works ("We always send artwork to China after licensor approval")
248	- exception_rule: An exception to a standard process ("For Walmart seasonal, sourcing sees it earlier")
249	- bottleneck: A constraint, delay, or dependency on a specific person/system/approval
250	- workaround: An unofficial solution someone built because the official system is insufficient
251	- system_limitation: What a system (Coldlion, ERP, email, spreadsheet) fails to do or show
252	- dependency: Something that must occur before something else can proceed
253	- handoff_gap: Information that tends to get lost between departments or people
254	- contradiction: Two conflicting statements about the same process
255	- process_ambiguity: Unclear ownership, unclear when something happens, or unclear who is responsible
256	
257	GROUP CHAT SEMANTICS — track how claims interact when multiple employees speak:
258	- claim_stated: First assertion of a new fact
259	- claim_confirmed: Another employee agrees
260	- claim_challenged: Another employee disagrees
261	- claim_refined: A nuance or caveat is added to a prior claim
262	- exception_introduced: An exception to a stated rule is revealed
263	- process_ambiguity_revealed: The conversation reveals that employees have conflicting understandings
264	
265	EXTRACTION RULES:
266	1. Only extract claims supported by what employees actually said.
267	2. exactQuote MUST be a verbatim substring of the source message — never paraphrase.
268	3. sourceMessageId must be one of the source IDs provided — for chat, use a message ID; for document ingestion, use a document chunk ID. Do not invent IDs.
269	4. Do NOT extract: pleasantries, greetings, Oracle assistant messages, or obvious generic facts with no operational specificity.
270	5. A single message may yield 0, 1, or multiple claims.
271	6. Set requiresReview=true if: impact >= 7, the claim names a specific person as a bottleneck, OR the claim implies customer or licensor risk.
272	7. Suggest gaps (follow-up questions) when the segment reveals uncertainty or ambiguity that needs resolution.
273	8. Do not flatten group conversation — if two employees express different views about the same process, extract both as separate claims with appropriate semantic roles and potentially a contradiction.
274	9. Classify claims by their operational meaning, not by whether a department name or keyword literally appears. A document can describe Licensing work without the word "licensing"; infer the relevant domain from responsibilities, approvals, handoffs, systems, and decisions described in the text.
275	10. Use the \`general\` domain for end-to-end business-process or companywide workflow claims that explain how work moves across multiple departments. Also include narrower domains such as \`licensing\`, \`design\`, \`production\`, \`sourcing\`, \`logistics\`, \`customers\`, \`sales\`, \`costing\`, or \`coldlion\` when the same claim is materially about those areas. \`costing\` means product/SKU costing and customer product pricing, not company finance/accounting. A costing sheet created by Design and sent to factories should usually include \`costing\` plus \`design\`, \`production\`, and/or \`sourcing\` if those handoffs are part of the claim.
276	11. Prefer small, reviewable operational claims over broad document summaries. Do not turn an entire form, spreadsheet, or SOP into one vague claim. Extract the concrete rule, handoff, responsibility, input, output, or exception.
277	12. For handoff claims, include every materially involved workflow domain. Example: "Design sends the costing sheet to factories so factories can quote production costs" is not just \`costing\`; it also involves \`design\` and \`sourcing\`/factory communication.
278	
279	ENTITY EXTRACTION RULES:
280	13. List every distinct entity the claim REFERENCES — not just the message-wide entities, but the ones THIS claim depends on.
281	14. The entityType values you may use are exactly: system, customer, licensor, factory, freight_provider, testing_lab, packaging_supplier, service_provider, vendor, person, sku_or_product_line, process_stage, department, geography, document_class.
282	15. \`licensor\` is reserved for entertainment / IP rights holders: Disney, Marvel, Star Wars, Lucasfilm, Warner Bros, NBCUniversal, etc. NEVER use \`vendor\` for those. \`vendor\` is the residual bucket for non-customer non-factory non-licensor business partners.
283	16. \`customer\` is the named retailer / buyer: Burlington, TJX, Ross, Hobby Lobby, Walmart, etc.
284	17. \`factory\` is an overseas manufacturer; \`freight_provider\` / \`testing_lab\` / \`packaging_supplier\` / \`service_provider\` are operational-vendor subtypes — pick the specific subtype if it fits, fall back to \`vendor\` only if none does.
285	18. \`system\` is software / tooling: Coldlion, ResourceSpace, Photoshop, Illustrator, Excel, Supabase, Email, WhatsApp, etc.
286	19. \`rawString\` should be how the entity appears in the message text — don't normalize to canonical form, the validator does that.
287	20. Omit \`proposedEntities\` entirely if the claim references no proper-noun entities.
288	
289	SENSITIVITY RULES — STRICT MODE:
290	21. Set \`sensitivityFlags\` ONLY when the content is clearly sensitive. Operational mentions of named people are NOT sensitive by themselves.
291	22. \`containsSensitiveHRData\` = true ONLY if the message describes: formal disciplinary actions (warnings, PIPs, terminations), compensation specifics (salary numbers, bonus amounts, raise discussions), formal performance reviews/ratings, or formally documented LOA reasons (medical leave, family leave). "Jordan is out next week" is NOT HR data. "We put Jordan on a final-written warning today" IS.
292	23. \`containsSensitivePersonalData\` = true ONLY for explicit personal information: medical conditions or diagnoses, family situations (divorce, deaths, dependents), legal issues, home addresses, personal contact details, or other clearly private personal facts.
293	24. \`isPersonalConflict\` = true ONLY for explicit interpersonal hostility BETWEEN NAMED INDIVIDUALS: shouting matches, harassment, character attacks, or formal complaints. Normal disagreement about a process is NOT a personal conflict.
294	25. If any flag fires, include a brief \`sensitivityReason\` naming which specific text triggered it. Omit \`sensitivityFlags\` entirely when nothing is sensitive — that's the common case.
295	26. When in doubt, do NOT set a flag. The cost of a false positive (admin reviews the quarantined candidate manually) is acceptable; the cost of a false negative (sensitive content reaches the knowledge graph) is not. But the threshold is "would a privacy/HR officer at this company call this sensitive?" — operational mentions of who works on what don't reach that bar.
296	
297	OUTPUT: Return only the structured JSON matching the schema. No narrative explanation outside the JSON.`;
298	
299	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
300	// Helper: format a conversation segment for the extraction model.
301	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
302	
303	export type FormattedMessage = {
304	  id: string;
305	  role: 'user' | 'assistant' | 'system';
306	  content: string;
307	  authorName: string | null;
308	  createdAt: Date;
309	};
310	
311	export interface FormatConversationSegmentOptions {
312	  carryIn?: FormattedMessage[];
313	}
314	
315	function appendFormattedMessages(lines: string[], msgs: FormattedMessage[]): void {
316	  for (const m of msgs) {
317	    if (m.role === 'system') continue;
318	    const speaker =
319	      m.role === 'assistant' ? '[Oracle - do not extract claims from this]' : `[${m.authorName ?? 'Unknown Employee'}]`;
320	    const timestamp = new Date(m.createdAt).toISOString().slice(0, 16).replace('T', ' ');
321	    lines.push(`Message ID: ${m.id}`);
322	    lines.push(`Speaker: ${speaker}`);
323	    lines.push(`Time: ${timestamp}`);
324	    lines.push(`Content: ${m.content}`);
325	    lines.push('---');
326	  }
327	}
328	
329	/**
330	 * Format a list of messages into a labeled conversation string for the extraction model.
331	 * Each message gets its ID, speaker, timestamp, and content clearly marked.
332	 */
333	export function formatConversationSegment(
334	  msgs: FormattedMessage[],
335	  options: FormatConversationSegmentOptions = {},
336	): string {
337	  const lines: string[] = [];
338	  const carryIn = options.carryIn?.filter((m) => m.role !== 'system') ?? [];
339	  if (carryIn.length > 0) {
340	    lines.push('CONTEXT ONLY - EARLIER MESSAGES:');
341	    lines.push('Use these messages only to interpret the active segment below.');
342	    lines.push('Do NOT extract claims from this context and do NOT quote these message IDs.');
343	    lines.push('---');
344	    appendFormattedMessages(lines, carryIn);
345	  }
346	  lines.push('CONVERSATION SEGMENT TO EXTRACT FROM:');
347	  lines.push('Extract claims only from the messages in this segment. Evidence quotes must use these message IDs only.');
348	  lines.push('---');
349	  appendFormattedMessages(lines, msgs);
350	  return lines.join('\n');
351	}
352

content.text: Now I understand the structure. The catalog here uses static routeIds, but the bake-off uses dynamic `provider/modelId` routes (e.g. `google/gemini-2.5-flash`). The spec said `catalog.ts:49` marks `gemini-2.5-flash` with `supportsReasoningControls:true` — but actually these dynamic routes are resolved in `resolve.ts`. Let me look at how dynamic routes get their `supportsReasoningControls` flag, and the route types. This is the crux of Bug 3.

content.content:
1	/**
2	 * Dynamic model route resolver.
3	 *
4	 * Handles two ID formats:
5	 *   1. Catalog routeId, e.g. "anthropic_claude_haiku_4_5_interview_primary"
6	 *   2. Provider/model id, e.g. "anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5"
7	 *
8	 * This resolver is synchronous and cannot read model_capabilities. Production
9	 * settings should use the DB-aware candidate resolver, which passes verified
10	 * capability flags into synthetic routes and enforces slot requirements.
11	 */
12	
13	import type { OracleModelRole, OracleModelRoute, OracleProvider, ReasoningEffort } from './types';
14	import { ORACLE_MODEL_ROUTES, getOracleRoute } from './catalog';
15	
16	const OR_PROVIDER_MAP: Record<string, OracleProvider> = {
17	  anthropic: 'anthropic',
18	  openai: 'openai',
19	  google: 'google',
20	  deepseek: 'deepseek',
21	  qwen: 'qwen',
22	};
23	
24	function mapPrefix(prefix: string): OracleProvider | null {
25	  return (OR_PROVIDER_MAP[prefix] ?? null) as OracleProvider | null;
26	}
27	
28	const SYNTHETIC_CAPS: Pick<
29	  OracleModelRoute,
30	  | 'supportsVision'
31	  | 'supportsStreaming'
32	  | 'supportsToolCalling'
33	  | 'supportsStructuredOutput'
34	  | 'supportsReasoningControls'
35	  | 'costTier'
36	  | 'enabled'
37	> = {
38	  supportsVision: true,
39	  supportsStreaming: true,
40	  supportsToolCalling: true,
41	  supportsStructuredOutput: true,
42	  supportsReasoningControls: false,
43	  costTier: 'balanced_default',
44	  enabled: true,
45	};
46	
47	export type SyntheticRouteCaps = Partial<typeof SYNTHETIC_CAPS>;
48	
49	function makeSyntheticRoute(
50	  providerModelId: string,
51	  provider: OracleProvider,
52	  modelId: string,
53	  role: OracleModelRole,
54	  caps?: SyntheticRouteCaps,
55	): OracleModelRoute {
56	  const cacheStrategy =
57	    provider === 'anthropic'
58	      ? 'anthropic_auto_plus_explicit'
59	      : provider === 'vertex'
60	        ? 'vertex_implicit_or_explicit_by_context_size'
61	        : provider === 'google'
62	          ? 'none'
63	          : provider === 'deepseek'
64	            ? 'deepseek_automatic_prefix'
65	            : provider === 'qwen'
66	              ? 'qwen_explicit_context_cache'
67	              : 'openai_automatic_with_cache_key';
68	
69	  const structuredOutputStrategy =
70	    provider === 'vertex' || provider === 'google'
71	      ? 'native_json_schema'
72	      : provider === 'openai'
73	        ? 'native_json_schema'
74	        : 'tool_call';
75	
76	  if (!caps) {
77	    console.warn(
78	      `[resolve] using synthetic route for "${providerModelId}" (role=${role}) without DB-verified capabilities. ` +
79	        `Production settings should use resolveRouteCandidates().`,
80	    );
81	  }
82	
83	  return {
84	    routeId: providerModelId,
85	    role,
86	    tier: 'primary',
87	    internalPurpose: null,
88	    provider,
89	    modelId,
90	    displayName: providerModelId,
91	    recommendedUse: `Dynamically resolved from model pool (${providerModelId}).`,
92	    cacheStrategy,
93	    structuredOutputStrategy,
94	    ...SYNTHETIC_CAPS,
95	    ...(caps ?? {}),
96	  };
97	}
98	
99	export function providerModelIdForRoute(route: OracleModelRoute): string {
100	  return `${route.provider}/${route.modelId}`;
101	}
102	
103	export function resolveModelRoute(
104	  modelIdOrRouteId: string,
105	  role: OracleModelRole,
106	  reasoningEffort?: ReasoningEffort,
107	  caps?: SyntheticRouteCaps,
108	): OracleModelRoute | null {
109	  let base: OracleModelRoute | null = null;
110	
111	  const byRouteId = getOracleRoute(modelIdOrRouteId);
112	  if (byRouteId) {
113	    base = byRouteId;
114	  } else {
115	    const slashIdx = modelIdOrRouteId.indexOf('/');
116	    if (slashIdx === -1) return null;
117	
118	    const prefix = modelIdOrRouteId.slice(0, slashIdx);
119	    const modelId = modelIdOrRouteId.slice(slashIdx + 1);
120	    const provider = mapPrefix(prefix);
121	    if (!provider) return null;
122	
123	    const catalogMatch = Object.values(ORACLE_MODEL_ROUTES).find(
124	      (r) => r.provider === provider && r.modelId === modelId && r.role === role,
125	    );
126	    base = catalogMatch ?? makeSyntheticRoute(modelIdOrRouteId, provider, modelId, role, caps);
127	  }
128	
129	  return reasoningEffort ? { ...base, reasoningEffort } : base;
130	}
131

1	/**
2	 * Oracle Model Route Types
3	 *
4	 * Per docs/oracle/01-model-roles-and-routes.md and docs/oracle/05-ai-retrofit-phase-packet.md.
5	 *
6	 * The Oracle exposes exactly 3 production roles. Runtime selection is driven by
7	 * admin settings and approved model pools; routes do not carry hidden fallback
8	 * targets.
9	 */
10	
11	export type OracleModelRole = 'interview' | 'extraction' | 'synthesis';
12	
13	export type OracleProvider = 'anthropic' | 'vertex' | 'google' | 'openai' | 'deepseek' | 'qwen';
14	
15	export type RouteTier =
16	  | 'primary'
17	  | 'fallback'
18	  | 'internal_subroute'
19	  | 'manual_only_frontier';
20	
21	export type RouteCostTier =
22	  | 'cheap_default'
23	  | 'balanced_default'
24	  | 'expensive_escalation'
25	  | 'manual_only_frontier';
26	
27	export type CacheStrategy =
28	  | 'anthropic_automatic'
29	  | 'anthropic_explicit_breakpoints'
30	  | 'anthropic_auto_plus_explicit'
31	  | 'vertex_implicit'
32	  | 'vertex_explicit_context_cache'
33	  | 'vertex_implicit_or_explicit_by_context_size'
34	  | 'openai_automatic_prefix'
35	  | 'openai_automatic_with_cache_key'
36	  | 'openai_automatic_with_retention'
37	  | 'deepseek_automatic_prefix'
38	  | 'qwen_explicit_context_cache'
39	  | 'qwen_none'
40	  | 'none';
41	
42	export type StructuredOutputStrategy =
43	  | 'native_json_schema'
44	  | 'tool_call'
45	  | 'schema_prompt_plus_validator';
46	
47	/**
48	 * Reasoning effort level — unified across providers. Each adapter translates
49	 * to its native form:
50	 *   Anthropic: thinking.budget_tokens (low=2048, medium=8192, high=24000; off omits the thinking param)
51	 *   OpenAI:    reasoning_effort string ('low'|'medium'|'high'; off omits the param)
52	 *   Vertex:    thinkingConfig.thinkingBudget (low=1024, medium=8192, high=24576; off=0)
53	 *   DeepSeek:  no-op (R1 reasoning is automatic and not client-controlled)
54	 *   Qwen:      enable_thinking + optional budget (low/med/high enable; off disables)
55	 */
56	export type ReasoningEffort = 'off' | 'low' | 'medium' | 'high';
57	
58	/**
59	 * Internal subroute purpose tags. These are NOT admin-selectable; they are
60	 * dispatched by OracleAIClient based on runtime conditions (sentiment, schema
61	 * repair, cheap triage pre-filter, etc.).
62	 */
63	export type InternalSubroutePurpose =
64	  | 'message_triage'        // Cheap pre-filter scout
65	  | 'warmth_escalation'     // Emotional-sensitivity route for frustrated/defensive sentiment
66	  | 'schema_repair';        // One-shot fix for malformed JSON candidates
67	
68	export interface OracleModelRoute {
69	  /** Stable identifier used in settings rows and observability. */
70	  routeId: string;
71	
72	  /** Which of the 3 production roles this route serves, or null for internal subroutes. */
73	  role: OracleModelRole | null;
74	
75	  /** Position in the strict 1-Primary / 1-Fallback / internal taxonomy. */
76	  tier: RouteTier;
77	
78	  /**
79	   * For internal subroutes only. Describes why OracleAIClient may dispatch to
80	   * this route at runtime. Null for primary/fallback production routes.
81	   */
82	  internalPurpose: InternalSubroutePurpose | null;
83	
84	  provider: OracleProvider;
85	  modelId: string;
86	  displayName: string;
87	  recommendedUse: string;
88	
89	  costTier: RouteCostTier;
90	  cacheStrategy: CacheStrategy;
91	  structuredOutputStrategy: StructuredOutputStrategy;
92	
93	  supportsVision: boolean;
94	  supportsStreaming: boolean;
95	  supportsToolCalling: boolean;
96	  supportsStructuredOutput: boolean;
97	  supportsReasoningControls: boolean;
98	
99	  maxInputTokens?: number;
100	  maxOutputTokens?: number;
101	
102	  /**
103	   * Reasoning effort to request from the model. Optional — when undefined,
104	   * the adapter omits all thinking/reasoning parameters and the model behaves
105	   * with its provider default. Set by resolveRouteFromSettings when the admin
106	   * has saved a per-stage effort preference.
107	   */
108	  reasoningEffort?: ReasoningEffort;
109	
110	  enabled: boolean;

content.text:
Now I fully understand Bug 3. The `supportsReasoningControls` for the dynamic `google/gemini-2.5-flash` route comes from `model_capabilities.thinking` in the DB. The model genuinely *can* think (text reasoning), so `thinking=true`, so the guard does nothing. The real root cause: **`gemini-2.5-flash` rejects the `thinkingLevel` enum field specifically** (the older `gemini-2.5` generation predates `thinkingLevel`; it uses the numeric `thinkingBudget`). The `thinkingLevel` enum is a Gemini 3.x feature.

Let me confirm this empirically against the live API before deciding the fix. First, let me check the verify harness and how to run things, plus check git baseline and env.

1	/**
2	 * VertexGeminiAdapter — direct Google Vertex AI integration via `@google/genai`.
3	 *
4	 * Architecture (DECISIONS.md D6, docs/oracle/02 §"Shared architecture"):
5	 * - Calls Vertex AI's REST API directly using Google's official GenAI SDK.
6	 * - NO Vercel AI SDK and NO OpenRouter in this path.
7	 * - Authenticates via Application Default Credentials. Set up locally with
8	 *   `gcloud auth application-default login`. In cloud runtime, mount a
9	 *   service-account JSON or use workload identity — the SDK auto-detects.
10	 * - Reads `GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT` and `GOOGLE_CLOUD_LOCATION` from env.
11	 *
12	 * Caching strategy:
13	 * - Implicit caching is automatic on Gemini's side; no client action needed.
14	 *   Cache hits show up as `usageMetadata.cachedContentTokenCount` and are
15	 *   normalized into `OracleUsage.cachedInputTokens`.
16	 * - For large reusable prefixes, this adapter creates explicit
17	 *   `cachedContent` resources, persists them through `provider_cached_content`,
18	 *   reuses them across worker processes by `source_hash`, and cleans them up
19	 *   once the hard TTL elapses.
20	 * - For oversized document artifacts, the adapter can upload a temporary cache
21	 *   source object to GCS and create the explicit cache from `fileData`
22	 *   instead of re-sending flattened text only.
23	 *
24	 * Structured output:
25	 * - Uses `responseMimeType: 'application/json'` + `responseJsonSchema:
26	 *   <jsonSchema>` (added in @google/genai 2.6). The JSON-schema mode is
27	 *   strict — Gemini enforces the schema on output. This is the direct
28	 *   capability that the OpenRouter -> Gemini bridge couldn't reach.
29	 *
30	 * Per docs/oracle/02-provider-native-ai-architecture.md → "Google Vertex AI
31	 * / Gemini direct".
32	 */

content.text: Let me check how Vertex handles thinking config (the known-good comparison), since Vertex uses `thinkingBudget` not `thinkingLevel`. This confirms my hypothesis on the proper Bug 3 fix.

This confirms it: **Vertex (which works with gemini-2.5-flash) uses `thinkingConfig.thinkingBudget` (numeric)** while the Google adapter uses `thinkingConfig.thinkingLevel` (enum). The `thinkingLevel` enum is a Gemini 3.x feature; gemini-2.5-flash rejects it. The proper fix: the Google adapter should emit `thinkingBudget` for models that use that field shape, and `thinkingLevel` for Gemini 3.x. This is a per-model capability of *how* thinking is expressed.

Let me verify this empirically against the live API now. First let me set up the environment and run a baseline. Let me check what keys are available and establish the git baseline.

This vault has `openai`, `gemini`, `dashscope`, `anthropic`, `deepseek` keys. There's no Vertex/Google service-account JSON here (`GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS_JSON`). 

The key strategic question: **Which auth path does prod Gemini use?** The HANDOFF says prod Trigger has `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS_JSON` set (service-account OAuth), and `google/*` routes resolve to `GoogleGeminiAdapter` which can mint OAuth tokens from that SA JSON. The `gemini` API key in 1Password is the Developer API path.

I have a decision to make about HOW to prove the fixes. There are two paths:

1. **Local live-API smokes** using 1Password keys (OpenAI, Gemini Developer API, DashScope) — fast, direct, proves the adapter code paths.
2. **End-to-end via deployed Trigger.dev worker** — proves prod reality (which uses SA OAuth for Gemini), required for definition-of-done anyway.

I'll do BOTH: local smokes to rapidly iterate/prove each adapter fix against live APIs, then deploy + e2e for the definition of done. For Gemini, prod uses the SA-OAuth REST path; the Developer API key path exercises the same `generateContent` request body (same `generationConfig` including the thinking config), so the thinkingLevel/thinkingBudget fix is validated on either path. I'll note the auth-path distinction in the report.

input.pattern: export function zodToJsonSchema|export function tryZodParse|export function parseJsonOrRaw|export function flattenPlan

content.text:
Now I understand `tryZodParse` THROWS on a real Zod failure (this is the validation path; important for Bug 1 — if `null` for an `.optional()` field fails Zod, the call throws). Let me verify: Zod `.optional()` accepts `undefined` but NOT `null`. So if OpenAI returns `null` for an optional field (which it must, since we make it required+nullable), `tryZodParse` will throw. This is exactly the Bug 1 subtlety. The fix per spec: map `.optional()` → `.nullish()` OR normalize null→undefined before validation. 

`.nullish()` is the single-source-of-truth fix and keeps the schema honest (optional fields may be null or absent), and it doesn't weaken other providers (Gemini/Vertex omit the field → undefined → still valid). Let me now write the verification harness. First, let me create the scratch env file with the keys, then build a comprehensive verify script.

content.text: Baseline typecheck passes cleanly (this includes the existing partial fixes). Now let me trace Bug 4 — the settings writer double-encoding. Let me find the model-picker and the API route it posts to.
The picker POSTs `value: valueToSave` (a bare string, NOT JSON-stringified) to `/api/admin/settings`. Now let me read the API route handler — that's where the double-encode likely happens.

This route writes `body.value` directly into the `settings.value` column. The key question: is `settings.value` a `jsonb` column? If so, Drizzle serializes the JS value (a string `"off"`) to JSON → stored as `"off"` (correct, single-encoded). So this route is NOT the double-encoder. Let me check the schema column type and look for OTHER writers (server actions, the `_set` bake-off tooling, page.tsx loader). The double-encode is likely a writer that calls `JSON.stringify` on an already-string value before insert.

content.text: `settings.value` is `jsonb`. So Drizzle auto-serializes a JS string. Writing the string `"off"` via Drizzle stores JSON `"off"` (single-encoded, correct). The double-encode `"\"off\""` happens when a writer passes an **already-JSON-stringified string** as the value. The reader (candidates.ts) reads `rows.get(effortKey)` and checks `isReasoningEffort(...)` which expects a bare string `'off'`. If the stored value is double-encoded `"\"off\""`, then `rows.get(effortKey)` returns the string `"off"` *with quotes* (i.e. JS string `'"off"'`), which fails `isReasoningEffort` → effort silently becomes `undefined`. That's the consumer-side symptom.

So: the canonical form is **bare** (jsonb stores the value, the JS string `'off'`). The writer that produced double-encoding must have passed `JSON.stringify('off')` = `'"off"'` as the value. The web route and seed and picker all pass bare values. The page.tsx loader reads `row.value` directly (the bare jsonb value) and passes it to the picker as `currentEffort`. So if the stored value is double-encoded `'"off"'`, the picker shows `'"off"'` (wrong). The consumer path expects bare.

Both the web POST route AND the picker AND the seed write bare values correctly. So the historical double-encoding must NOT have come from these app paths under normal operation — it likely came from a **manual SQL write or a script** that did `UPDATE settings SET value = '"off"'` (a JSON-string literal in SQL is itself a JSON string, so `'"off"'::jsonb` = the JSON string `"off"`, which is CORRECT)... 

Wait — let me reconsider. The actual double-encode `"\"off\""` means the jsonb value is the JSON string containing `"off"` WITH quotes. In SQL that's `'"\"off\""'::jsonb` or, more likely, someone did `UPDATE settings SET value = to_jsonb('"off"'::text)` or passed `JSON.stringify('off')` as the value through a path that ALSO jsonb-encodes. 

The key realization: **the web POST route is actually fine for normal `value: 'off'`. But the model-picker's effort save and the bake-off SQL are not the only writers.** Let me check git history for the actual fix that was applied to the data, and the new migration file `79`, which will reveal exactly what the corrupted shape was and confirm the canonical form.

This migration was authored by a prior session. The corrupted shape was a **double-encoded empty string** `""` (jsonb holding the 2-char string `""`). That means a writer stored `JSON.stringify("")` = `'""'` as the jsonb value. 

Where would an empty-string effort come from? The model-picker: `effort` state defaults to `currentEffort ?? 'medium'`, but the save only fires the effort write `if (effortSettingKey && selectedModel?.thinking)`. So that's not empty either. 

Let me reconsider the route handler. `UpsertSchema.value` is `z.unknown()`. If a client ever POSTs `value: '""'` or the body's value arrives pre-stringified... Actually, the more likely culprit: a writer that does `db.insert(settings).values({ value: JSON.stringify(x) })` where the column is jsonb — that double-encodes. content.text: Those `JSON.stringify` calls are all `fetch` body serialization (correct, that's the HTTP body, not the DB value). The `value` field inside each body is a bare value. Let me check `dispatch-mode-toggle.tsx` and `model-pool-editor.tsx` — those POST to the same `/api/admin/settings`. Let me see exactly what `value` they send, and crucially examine the bug: is there any path where `value` itself is pre-stringified?

All app writers send bare `value`. So under the CURRENT app code, the double-encode cannot occur through the UI. The `79` migration's comment confirms it was a **historical one-off** (a double-encoded empty string), and the value-repair is done.

The spec's Bug 4 mandate is: "find and fix the WRITER, make writes idempotent, add a regression guard." Since the current app writers are already correct (bare value → jsonb → single-encoded), the real risk is **the route handler accepting an already-encoded value without guarding**, and there being **no regression guard**. The proper, permanent fix per the "never a band-aid, make writer idempotent + add guard" mandate is:

1. **Harden the writer (`/api/admin/settings` POST)** to be idempotent against double-encoding: if `value` arrives as a string that is itself valid JSON-encoding of a primitive (i.e. a string that starts/ends with quotes like `'"off"'` or `'""'`), detect and unwrap it — so a write of an already-encoded value stores the canonical single-encoded form. This makes ALL writers (including future scripts/manual API calls) idempotent.
2. **Add a regression guard** (`__verify__`) that scans the settings table (or asserts on the encoding logic) and fails if any scalar settings value is double-encoded.

But I must be careful: settings values are heterogeneous (strings, numbers, booleans, arrays). I should only normalize the specific double-encode pathology: a JSON value that is a *string* whose content is itself a JSON-encoded scalar. Actually the safest, most correct rule that can't corrupt legitimate string values: detect when an incoming `value` is a **string that parses as JSON to a non-string scalar or to a string** — that's ambiguous. 

Let me reconsider to avoid over-engineering. The cleanest root-cause-correct approach: the double-encode comes from a value being `JSON.stringify`'d before being handed to the jsonb writer. The truly idempotent fix at the writer is: **if `value` is a string AND that string is a syntactically valid JSON document whose re-parse yields a string/number/boolean/null (a scalar), unwrap one layer.** This precisely reverses an accidental double-encode while leaving normal model-id strings (e.g. `"google/gemini-2.5-flash"` — NOT valid JSON, since it has no quotes) untouched. A normal model id like `google/gemini-2.5-flash` is not valid JSON so it won't be unwrapped. A value like `"off"` (bare) is not valid JSON either (no quotes) → untouched. Only `'"off"'` (with embedded quotes) parses as JSON string `off` → unwrapped to `off`. 

This is exact and safe. Let me verify my understanding of the data shapes by reading current prod settings first (Bug 4 acceptance requires round-trip), and record the baseline prod settings (required before any mutation). Let me get the prod pooler URL.

content.text:
**Critical finding for Bug 4.** The double-encoding is REAL and still present in prod, beyond just the reasoning-effort key:

- `model_pool_interview` and `model_pool_synthesis` are stored as **double-encoded JSON strings** (jsonb holds a *string* that contains the JSON array text, instead of a jsonb array). 
- `model_pool_extraction` and `model_pool_vision`... extraction is a proper array (good). 

This confirms the writer bug affects **arrays** too, and is live in prod right now. The `candidates.ts` reader does `Array.isArray(rows.get(poolKey))` — for the double-encoded pools, `rows.get(poolKey)` is a *string*, not an array, so `pool` becomes `[]` → `NoConfiguredModelError` ("pool is empty") for interview/synthesis if those were used with enforcement. This is a latent production bug.

Now I can see the pattern precisely: some writer `JSON.stringify`'d the array before passing to the jsonb column. The model-pool-editor sends `value: Array.from(pools[stage])` (a real array) — correct. So these double-encoded pools came from an older writer or a script. Regardless, the **permanent fix** is to make the writer idempotent (unwrap accidental double-encoding) AND add a guard. This also lets me repair the live prod data correctly.

Now I have full understanding of all 4 bugs. Let me mark this chapter and start implementing. Let me write the verification harness first so I can prove fixes as I make them.

content.text:
All consumers use null-safe access (`?.` and `?? []` / `?? false` / `?? null`). `null` and `undefined` both work identically with `?.` and `??`. So `.nullish()` is fully compatible downstream. 

Now the **TypeScript type** concern: `.nullish()` changes the inferred type from `T | undefined` to `T | null | undefined`. The consumers use `?.`/`??` so they're fine, but let me verify the `ExtractedClaim` type used in `document-ingestion.ts` is derived from this schema (it is — `ExtractionClaim = z.infer<...>`). The `(extracted.proposedEntities ?? [])` handles null. Good. Typecheck will confirm.

Now **Bug 1 part 2**: update the OpenAI `walk()` to make formerly-optional properties nullable (union type with null), not just force them required. Let me rewrite `walk()`.

content.text: Now update `walk()` to apply `makeNullable` to properties that were not in the original `required` set.

content.text:
Now there's a subtle ordering issue: `walk` recurses into `obj.properties` values via `for (const value of Object.values(obj)) walk(value)`. When it recurses into a property that I just made nullable via `anyOf: [rest, {type:'null'}]`, it will walk the `{type:'null'}` branch and `rest`. That's fine. But there's a concern: a nested object property that becomes `anyOf:[rest,...]` where `rest` contains `properties` — walk will still recurse and fix `required` there. Good.

One more concern: `makeNullable` for the `sensitivityFlags` field. In Zod, `sensitivityFlags` is `ExtractionSensitivityFlagsSchema.nullish()` — an object. Its JSON schema node has `type: 'object'` with `properties`. `makeNullable` turns `type:'object'` → `type:['object','null']`. Then walk recurses into it and forces its inner `required` to all keys + makes its inner optional (`sensitivityReason`) nullable. Good — that's correct and OpenAI accepts it.

content.text:
Now, before running, I must implement the **Bug 3 fix in the Google adapter** (currently it always emits `thinkingLevel`). The fix: emit `thinkingBudget` (numeric) for Gemini 2.x models and `thinkingLevel` (enum) for Gemini 3.x+. This is "how a model expresses thinking" — a capability that should be driven by metadata, not a hard-coded model name. 

The cleanest catalog-driven approach: add an optional field to `OracleModelRoute` describing the thinking-control *style* (`thinkingBudget` vs `thinkingLevel`), defaulting based on a metadata signal. But the dynamic routes get caps from `model_capabilities`, which only has a boolean `thinking`. I need a way to determine the *style* without hard-coding the model id in adapter logic.

Let me reconsider the truly correct design. The thinking-control style is a property of the **model generation/API**, knowable from the model id pattern but that's "model-specific in the adapter" which the owner forbids *in adapter logic*. The right place: encode it as a route capability field derived in the catalog/capability layer. Let me look at how `model_capabilities` is sourced to see if there's a natural place, and check the openrouter/capability types.

The `thinking` flag is a boolean; there's no "thinking style" column, and adding one to the DB would require a migration plus catalog-refresh changes — heavyweight and the DB capability source (OpenRouter) doesn't provide it. 

The right architectural answer that satisfies "no hard-coded model names in adapter logic, model facts live in the catalog/capability layer and flow through the route": **encode the thinking-control style as a derived route field, computed at route-construction time (the catalog/resolve layer), from the model id — NOT inside the adapter's request-building logic.** The adapter just reads `route.thinkingControlStyle` and emits the corresponding field. The *derivation* (which is inherently model-id-based) lives in the route/capability layer where model facts belong, exactly like `structuredOutputStrategy` and `cacheStrategy` are already derived from provider in `makeSyntheticRoute`.

Let me add a `geminiThinkingStyle` field to the route type, derive it in `resolve.ts` (catalog layer) for google/vertex providers, and have the adapter consume it. Let me add the type first.

input.new_string:
export type StructuredOutputStrategy =
  | 'native_json_schema'
  | 'tool_call'
  | 'schema_prompt_plus_validator';

/**
 * How a Gemini-family model expresses reasoning control on the Google Developer
 * API (`generativelanguage`). This is a model-generation fact, not an adapter
 * decision, so it lives on the route (derived in the catalog/resolve layer):
 *
 *   - 'thinking_budget' — Gemini 2.x: `thinkingConfig.thinkingBudget` (integer
 *     token budget). The 2.x generation REJECTS the `thinkingLevel` enum with a
 *     400 "Thinking level is not supported for this model" (this 400 is what
 *     broke gemini-2.5-flash vision when any reasoning effort was set).
 *   - 'thinking_level' — Gemini 3.x+: `thinkingConfig.thinkingLevel` enum
 *     (LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH). The 3.x generation does not accept a raw thinkingBudget.
 *   - 'none' — model does not support client-controlled thinking; omit entirely.
 *
 * The Vertex adapter has always used thinkingBudget and is unaffected; this flag
 * exists for the Google Developer-API adapter, which previously hard-coded the
 * thinkingLevel enum for every model.
 */
export type GeminiThinkingStyle = 'thinking_budget' | 'thinking_level' | 'none';

content.text:
Wait — I need to reconsider the recursion. If a legitimate value is the bare string `"off"` (JS string `off`, no quotes), `looksEncoded` is false → returned as-is. Good. If it's `'"off"'` (JS string with quotes), `JSON.parse` → `off` (JS string without quotes) → recurse → `off` not encoded → return `off`. Correct. If it's a legit JSON array passed as a real JS array (not a string), `typeof !== 'string'` → returned as-is. Correct.

But one risk: a legitimate scalar string value that happens to be valid JSON, e.g. a description or a model id that is numeric-like. Model ids contain `/` and letters — never valid JSON. Efforts (`off`/`low`/`medium`/`high`), dispatch (`sync`/`batch`), `enforce` (`true`/`false` booleans, not strings) — none are JSON-encoded strings. The only values that `looksEncoded` catches are genuinely wrapped ones. This is safe. Let me apply `normalizeSettingValue` in the POST handler.

content.text: Now the **regression guard**. I'll add a pure unit-style `__verify__` in `@oracle/ai` that exercises the normalization logic and asserts the round-trip invariant, so it runs in the gates without DB access. But `normalizeSettingValue` lives in the web route. Better: extract the normalization into a shared, importable helper so both the web route and the guard use one source of truth. content.text: One assertion reveals a real edge-case decision: the bare string `"24000"` (which doesn't start with quote/bracket) is NOT caught by `looksEncoded`, so it stays the string `"24000"` — not the number. That's actually **correct behavior**: a bare numeric string `"24000"` is ambiguous and we should NOT coerce it (the writer should send a real number). My test assertion was wrong, not the code. A bare `"24000"` has no JSON wrapper, so leaving it as-is is the safe, correct choice. Let me fix the test expectation.

content.text: `legacyStanceToCandidateStance(extracted.semanticRole)` expects `string | undefined` but now gets `... | null`. The cleanest fix that matches the existing "null means absent" semantics: coalesce `null` to `undefined` at the call site (`?? undefined`). Let me check the function signature first to choose the right fix.

The function takes `string | undefined`. I'll widen its parameter to accept `null` (cleanest — it already handles unknown roles via the switch default returning null). This is more robust than coalescing at one call site.

model_runs => id, task_type, model, provider, prompt_version, input_hash, input_tokens, output_tokens, cost_usd, latency_ms, success, error, created_at, dispatch_mode

content.text: `model_runs` DOES have `model`, `provider`, `cost_usd`. The error was because `model_run_attempts` uses `model_id`/`status` not `model`/`success`. Let me fix the collector.

**M1 complete and rich.** gpt-4o-mini: extraction success, 7311 in / 1247 out tokens, 14.2s, returned 9 claims, 6 promoted (3 process_rule, 2 handoff_gap, 1 dependency). Vision qwen3-vl: 9620 chars / 66 edges transcription. Let me view the claim dump to assess quality, then move to M2.

content.content:
1 [dependency] After the Buyer schedules a meeting, the Sales team prepares an agenda template for the kickoff meeting.
    Q: [Buyer: "Meeting Scheduled"] --> [Sales: "Agenda template/ Kick off Meeting"]
2 [handoff_gap] The Sales team confirms the buyer's approval before the Creative direction team can proceed with costing sheets approval.
    Q: [Factories: "Price"] --(If Sales confirms buyer approves the Price)--> [Creative direction: "Costing sheets ap
3 [handoff_gap] The Creative direction team must submit the Tech Pack for authorization before the Junior designers can approve it.
    Q: [Creative direction: "Tech Pack Submit Authorization"] --> [Junior designers: "Tech Pack Approval"]
4 [process_rule] The Licensing Team submits the PPS to the licensor after the Production team reviews the audit and sends it to the factory.
    Q: [Gina: "Review Audit and send to factory"] --(If Audit: Pass)--> [Licensing Team: "PPS Submit to licensor"]
5 [process_rule] The Technical designers elaborate on costing sheets before uploading them to DFlow with the RFQ code.
    Q: [Technical designers: "Costing sheets elaboration"] --> [Technical designers: "Upload to DFlow RFQ code"]
6 [process_rule] The Production team orders samples and requests the Tech Pack after including it in the task list.
    Q: [Production: "Include in Task List"] --> [Production: "Samples Order / TechPack request"]
